coredata.dc:description: Radiotherapy is a cornerstone in the treatment of malignant tumors, yet its efficacy is often hindered by intrinsic and acquired resistance mechanisms, notably limited radiation availability and tumor hypoxia within inner tumor region. This study introduces a multifunctional nanosensitizer, CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP, with high tumor accumulation and deep penetration capabilities via dynamic conformational change. CDBi,Mn-FA was bismuth manganese-doped carbon dots, offering radiosensitizing properties and catalase-like activity for oxygen generation. Folic acid modification further enhances tumor cell targeting. These carbon dots are encapsulated in calcium phosphate nanoparticles to form CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP, which demonstrates improved in vivo circulation and tumor accumulation via the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect. Upon encountering the acidic tumor microenvironment, the encapsulation degrades, releasing smaller CDBi,Mn-FA particles that deeply penetrate tumor tissues. In vitro and in vivo experiments establish that CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP effectively targets tumor tissue, enhances radiotherapy-induced apoptosis, and suppresses tumor growth. Notably, the nanoparticle achieves a 40 % tumor cure rate and ensures 100 % survival within 45 days post-treatment. Biocompatibility studies confirm minimal systemic impact, with efficient renal and fecal elimination, indicating promising clinical translational potential. This study introduces a novel radiation therapy sensitization strategy and underscores the potential of such size changeable nanoparticles as an advanced targeted drug delivery system for tumors.

head.ce:title: Bismuth/manganese-doped carbon dots in calcium phosphate matrix: Dynamic conformational nanoparticles enhancing tumor accumulation, deep penetration, and radiosensitivity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Radiotherapy is a cornerstone in the treatment of malignant tumors, yet its efficacy is often hindered by intrinsic and acquired resistance mechanisms, notably limited radiation availability and tumor hypoxia within inner tumor region. This study introduces a multifunctional nanosensitizer, CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP, with high tumor accumulation and deep penetration capabilities via dynamic conformational change. CDBi,Mn-FA was bismuth manganese-doped carbon dots, offering radiosensitizing properties and catalase-like activity for oxygen generation. Folic acid modification further enhances tumor cell targeting. These carbon dots are encapsulated in calcium phosphate nanoparticles to form CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP, which demonstrates improved in vivo circulation and tumor accumulation via the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect. Upon encountering the acidic tumor microenvironment, the encapsulation degrades, releasing smaller CDBi,Mn-FA particles that deeply penetrate tumor tissues. In vitro and in vivo experiments establish that CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP effectively targets tumor tissue, enhances radiotherapy-induced apoptosis, and suppresses tumor growth. Notably, the nanoparticle achieves a 40 % tumor cure rate and ensures 100 % survival within 45 days post-treatment. Biocompatibility studies confirm minimal systemic impact, with efficient renal and fecal elimination, indicating promising clinical translational potential. This study introduces a novel radiation therapy sensitization strategy and underscores the potential of such size changeable nanoparticles as an advanced targeted drug delivery system for tumors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Radiotherapy remains a cornerstone in the treatment of malignant tumors, with more than 50 % of cancer patients requiring this intervention during their treatment course.[1,2] This modality primarily exerts its effects by directly damaging biomolecules, predominantly DNA, within cells or indirectly through the generation of free radicals by high-energy ionizing radiation, leading to cell apoptosis or necrosis and ultimately achieving the destruction of malignant tumors.[3–6] However, the efficacy of radiotherapy is often compromised by the development of resistance, either intrinsic or acquired, during the course of treatment.[7,8] This resistance arises from two main factors: the limited availability of radiation into the deep layers of tumor tissues and the resultant intrinsic resistance of cancer cells in these regions,[9] and the hypoxic environment within the deeper tumor tissues, which reduces the efficiency of free radical generation, thereby leading to adaptive resistance to radiotherapy.[10] These resistance mechanisms result in incomplete tumor eradication during clinical treatment, contributing to tumor recurrence and metastasis. Therefore, enhancing the radiosensitivity of deep-seated tumors is crucial for achieving complete radiotherapy outcomes.

To overcome this challenge, various radiotherapy sensitization strategies have been developed, including the use of radiosensitizers and oxygen generators.[11] Radiosensitizers function by increasing the photon capture cross-section of radiation and utilizing the Compton scattering effect to enhance the deposition of high-energy radiation in tumor cells, thereby sensitizing them to radiotherapy.[12,13] High-Z elements are commonly employed as radiosensitizers due to their superior photon capture capabilities.[3,14,15] However, hypoxic tumor cells exhibit resistance to radiation that is 2–3 times higher than that of normoxic cells[16]. Consequently, maintaining oxygen supply during radiotherapy sensitization is essential to enhance the therapeutic efficacy against hypoxic tumor cells. Various strategies have been reported to alleviate hypoxia, including the use of perfluorocarbons to carry oxygen and the application of mild photothermal effects to increase blood flow to the tumor site.[17–20] Among these methods, catalytic oxygen generation is recognized as being more persistent and efficient. Studies have shown that certain metal-based materials possess peroxidase-like activity, enabling them to catalyze the degradation of H2O2 in the tumor microenvironment to produce oxygen in situ,[21–24] thus relieving hypoxia. Therefore, combining high-Z elements with metal elements that possess catalytic oxygen generation properties to construct multifunctional nanoparticle radiosensitizers holds significant potential for enhancing the radiosensitivity of deep-seated tumors.

The effective enrichment of nano-radiosensitizers at the tumor site is a prerequisite for their therapeutic efficacy, and targeted delivery is a multi-step process. Nanoparticles require prolonged blood circulation to reduce non-specific liver and kidney clearance, allowing them to accumulate in tumor tissues and achieve deep penetration.[25,26] Research has indicated that the particle size of nanoparticles significantly impacts their tumor-targeted enrichment and penetration abilities. Nanoparticles that are too small are rapidly excreted by the kidneys,[27] while those that are too large are recognized and captured by the reticuloendothelial system (RES) and subsequently cleared by the liver.[28] Previous studies have shown that nanoparticles with a size of approximately 100 nm exhibit favorable in vivo circulation and an enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect.[29,30] However, the EPR effect primarily facilitates the accumulation of nanomedicines at the tumor periphery, whereas ultra-small nanoparticles (below 10 nm) demonstrate superior tumor penetration capabilities.[31,32] Therefore, the size requirements for the targeted delivery and deep penetration of nano-radiosensitizers are contradictory: larger particle sizes are necessary for prolonged circulation and tumor enrichment via the EPR effect, while smaller particle sizes are required for overcoming high interstitial fluid pressure and achieving deep tumor penetration.[33–35].

To address these conflicting requirements, we designed dynamic conformational nanoparticles with tumor microenvironment-responsive characteristics, enabling a transition in nanoparticle size from large to small to satisfy the distinct needs for tumor enrichment and deep penetration (Scheme 1 ). We initially synthesized bismuth manganese-doped carbon dots (CDBi,Mn-FA) with particle sizes less than 5 nm using a hydrothermal method. Bismuth,[36] being the highest molecular weight high-Z element, provides excellent radiosensitizing properties, while manganese doping imparts catalase-like activity to the carbon dots, catalyzing the degradation of H2O2 to produce oxygen and alleviate hypoxia. Folic acid was incorporated during synthesis to enable CDBi,Mn-FA to specifically recognize tumor cells overexpressing folate receptors. Subsequently, CDBi,Mn-FA was encapsulated in calcium phosphate nanoparticles via biomineralization, forming CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP particles with an approximate diameter of 100 nm. Compared to CDBi,Mn-FA, CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP exhibits significantly prolonged in vivo circulation and tumor site accumulation through the EPR effect. Upon reaching the tumor tissue, the calcium phosphate component rapidly degrades in response to the slightly acidic tumor microenvironment, releasing smaller CDBi,Mn-FA particles, thereby facilitating deep tumor penetration and radiosensitization. Combined with low-dose radiotherapy, CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP effectively inhibits tumor growth, prolongs the survival of tumor-bearing mice, and demonstrates excellent biological safety and in vivo metabolic characteristics, highlighting its potential for clinical translation.

Glucose monohydrate (C6H12O6·H2O) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Folic acid (C19H19N7O6) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Bismuth potassium citrate (C12H10BiK3O14) and Manganese(II) citrate (C12H10Mn3O14) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Cellulose dialysis bag (1000 Da) was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co. Ltd. Calcium chloride (CaCl2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The microstructural and surface state of CDs were characterized by typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL/JEM-F200), X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku Ultima IV), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Thermo Scientific iN10), UV/Vis absorbance spectroscopy (Shimadzu UV-3600), Photoluminescence spectra (Edinburgh FLS1000).

The CDBi-FA were obtained by one-step hydrothermal method. Specifically, 0.55 g glucose monohydrate (C6H12O6·H2O), 0.57 g folic acid (C19H19N7O6) and 0.57 g bismuth potassium citrate (C12H10BiK3O14) were dissolved in 60 mL deionized water under constant ultrasound. Then the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 12 h. After that, the reaction solution was cooled down to the room temperature naturally, the obtained product was centrifuged for 3 mins at the speed of 6000 rpm until the large size particles to be removed. Then, the CDBi-FA purification was further performed by dialyzing in a dialysis bag for 3 days, followed by freeze-dried to obtain brown powder (CDBi-FA). The CDBi,Mn were synthesized via 0.55 g glucose monohydrate (C6H12O6·H2O), 0.57 g Manganese(II) citrate (C12H10Mn3O14), 0.57 g bismuth potassium citrate (C12H10BiK3O14), and 60 mL deionized water. The CDBi,Mn-FA were synthesized via 0.55 g glucose monohydrate (C6H12O6·H2O), 0.57 g folic acid (C19H19N7O6), 0.57 g Manganese(II) citrate (C12H10Mn3O14), 0.57 g bismuth potassium citrate (C12H10BiK3O14), and 60 mL deionized water. Other steps were in accordance with CDBi-FA.

Incubate 1 mL of 1 mg/mL CDBi,Mn-FA solution with 10 μL of 1 mol/L CaCl2 solution, then add to phosphate-rich cell complete medium (DMEM medium containing 10 % FBS), stir in the dark for 40 min, under bionic conditions, synthesize calcium phosphate-loaded CDBi,Mn-FA nanoparticles (CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP NPs). Centrifuge at 16000 rpm, wash three times with ultrapure water, ultrasound with 90 Hz ultrasound probe for 10 s, lyophilized for standby. The size distribution and zeta potential were determined using the Malvern Zeta Sizer Nano series (Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments, UK). The morphology was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Titan G2-F20, FEI, USA).

The prepared nanoparticles were dispersed in H2O, 10 %FBS-contained PBS, and 10 %FBS-contained DMEM, and the samples were taken out at the corresponding time points at 0 h, 1 h, 3 h, 6 h, 12 h, 24 h, and 48 h to measure the particle size, and observe the changes of particle size.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of pH response of nanoparticles.
ce:section.ce:para: Investigating the degradation of nanoparticles at different pH. The prepared nanoparticles were centrifuged, dispersed in PBS (10 mM, pH 6.5) and PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) solutions, incubated at 37 °C, centrifuged at 0 h, 0.5 h, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h to take the supernatant for use. The Ca2+ content in the supernatant was detected, and the color change of the nanoparticle solution was observed. Samples were taken at 5 min and 30 min, and the morphological changes of the nanoparticles were observed by TEM.

In this study, Human Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cells (HUVEC) and MDA-MB-231 cells were obtained from the Xiangya Cell Center in Changsha, China. The human hepatocellular carcinoma cells (HuH7), human colorectal adenocarcinoma cells (SW480), and human breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231) were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % penicillin–streptomycin. These cells were incubated in a controlled environment at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 to mimic natural physiological conditions and promote healthy cell proliferation.

Fresh tumor tissue is obtained from breast cancer patients through surgery or biopsy and immediately placed in pre-cooled basal medium with antibiotics to prevent contamination. Under sterile conditions, the tumor tissue is cut into 1–2 mm3 pieces and digested with Tumor Tissue Digestion Solution (Cat: MB-0818L05L, Mogengel Biotechnology) at 37 °C for 1–2 h, with gentle shaking to ensure uniform digestion. After digestion, the solution is filtered through a 40–100 µm cell strainer to remove undigested tissue fragments, collecting single-cell suspensions and small clusters. The cell suspension is mixed with Matrigel at an appropriate ratio and distributed onto culture plates, adding 5–10 µL of the mixture per well, followed by incubation at 37 °C for 10–20 min to allow gelation. Pre-warmed organoid culture medium (Cat: OG01-HC, Mogengel Biotechnology) is then added to cover the Matrigel (Cat: 082755, Mogengel Biotechnology), and the plates are incubated at 37 °C with 5 % CO2, and The medium is replaced every 2–3 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability assay of breast cancer organoids
ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer organoid were seeded into 10 μL of Matrigel in a 96-well culture plate and incubated for 24 h. The cells were then treated under different conditions for 3 days, and cell viability was assessed using the Alamar Blue kit (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.). Fluorescence (emission wavelength: 585 nm) was measured using a microplate reader (Infinite M200 PRO, TECAN, Austria).

MDA-MB-231, HuH7 and SW480 cells were seeded at a density of 5,000 cells per well in 96-well plates and incubated at 37 °C under normoxic (21 % O2) or hypoxic (1 % O2) conditions to reach 60 % confluence. Subsequently, the medium was replaced with one containing varying concentrations of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP (0 to 50 μg/mL), followed by a further 6-hour incubation. Cells were then subjected to X-ray radiation (225kv, 7.2mv, 1.0 Gy/min) across a range of doses (0 to 16 Gy). Cell viability was quantitatively assessed using a Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay; after adding the CCK-8 solution as per manufacturer's guidelines, plates were incubated for 2 h, and absorbance was measured at 450 nm with a microplate reader (Infinite M200 PRO, TECAN, Austria).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxygen production capacity of H2O2 catalyzed by nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Take the same concentration of CDBi,Mn-FA and CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP (20 μg/mL, based on Mn concentration) and add H2O2 (100 mM) to determine the catalytic oxygen production effect under pH 6.5 and pH 7.4, and the oxygen generation was monitored every 10 s for 600 s. The determining catalytic oxygen production capacity by measuring oxygen production using the portable dissolved oxygen meter (JPBJ-609L, INESA Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., China).

Digested logarithmic growth phase HUVEC cells and MDA-MB-231 cells were inoculated in 6-well plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells/well, and the cells were wall-adhered after 24 h of incubation, 1 mg/mL FA was added to one of the wells for saturating the folate receptor on the surface of the cells, and after 4 h of incubation, the medium was discarded, and the medium without FBS was reintroduced, and to the wells where FA was added CDBi,Mn-FA, and the remaining two wells were sequentially added CDBi,Mn, CDBi,Mn-FA, respectively, and incubated for 4 h. After incubation, the cells were washed with PBS for three times, and the uptake of nanoparticles by cells was observed by confocal laser microscopy (CLSM), after which the fluorescence intensity of the cells was detected by flow cytometry.

Digest HUVEC cells and MDA-MB-231 cells in the logarithmic growth phase, stain MDA-MB-231 cells with cell membrane dye Dil, wash them thoroughly, wash off excess dye, inoculate HUVEC cells and MDA-MB-231 cells at a density of 5 × 104 cells/well in a 6-well plate, let stand in a cell culture tank for 12 h, then add CDBi,Mn-FA and incubate for 4 h, then wash them with PBS for 3 times, and observe the uptake of nanoparticles by the two co-incubated cells with laser confocal microscopy (CLSM).

MDA-MB-231 cells were seeded in 6-well plates without the need to reach 60 % confluence, under normoxic or hypoxic conditions. After cell adherence, the culture medium was substituted with one containing 50 μg/mL of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP, followed by a 6-hour incubation. Cells were then irradiated with 4 Gy of X-ray. Post-irradiation, cells were re-seeded at low densities to facilitate colony formation. After 10–14 days, colonies were fixed and stained with crystal violet. Quantitative analysis of the colony area was performed using Image J software, allowing for a semi-quantitative assessment of clonogenic survival under both oxygen conditions, conducted in triplicate to ensure statistical rigor.

In our investigation, MDA-MB-231 cells were initially cultured in 96-well plates, where they were allowed to adhere overnight. Subsequently, these cells were subjected to hypoxic conditions and treated with 50 μg/mL CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP, followed by exposure to 4 Gy X-ray radiation to simulate stress conditions. To visualize cell nuclei and detect reactive oxygen species (ROS), a two-step staining procedure was employed. Firstly, for nuclear staining, cells underwent a wash with PBS before incubation with Hoechst dye solution for 15 min at room temperature in the dark, aiming to label the nuclei for subsequent morphological analysis and cell count. Following this, cells were washed again with PBS to eliminate any residual Hoechst dye and then treated with DCFH-DA, a fluorescent dye that penetrates cell membranes. Inside the cell, DCFH-DA is converted by cellular esterases into a non-fluorescent compound, which, upon oxidation by ROS, transforms into the highly fluorescent DCF. This allows for the detection of ROS when cells are incubated with DCFH-DA for 30 min at 37 °C in darkness. After staining, a final wash with PBS was performed to remove unbound dye, ensuring the specificity of the fluorescence signal to ROS levels within the cells. The stained cells were then observed under a confocal microscope, using appropriate filters to distinguish between the blue fluorescence of Hoechst for nuclei and the green fluorescence of DCF indicating ROS. The captured images were analyzed with image analysis software to quantify DCF fluorescence intensity.

Leveraging the Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) Assay Kit for precise ROS quantification, MDA-MB-231 cells were treated with 50 μg/mL CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP under normoxic or hypoxic conditions, followed by exposure to 4 Gy of X-ray radiation. Subsequent to treatment, the DCFH-DA probe from the kit, which is initially non-fluorescent, was utilized. This probe diffuses into cells, where intracellular esterases convert it to DCFH, which is then oxidized by ROS to the fluorescent DCF, enabling the quantification of ROS levels. After washing off the excess probe, cells were subjected to flow cytometry (FACSVerse, BD, USA) analysis. This analysis was performed by employing a 488 nm excitation wavelength and a 525 nm emission filter, settings that are compatible with the fluorescence characteristics of DCF.

Protein samples are initially collected and subjected to electrophoresis for separation, specifically isolating γ-H2AX (Cat. ab11174) and HIF-1α (Cat. ab51608) proteins. Following separation, proteins are transferred onto a membrane, which is subsequently blocked with a solution to prevent nonspecific binding. Primary antibodies targeting γ-H2AX and HIF-1α are applied to bind the respective proteins. This step is followed by the introduction of secondary antibodies conjugated with an enzyme. Upon adding a chemiluminescent substrate, the reaction between the substrate and the enzyme-conjugated secondary antibodies facilitates the detection of protein bands. Finally, ImageJ software is utilized for quantitative analysis of the bands, enabling precise measurement of γ-H2AX and HIF-1α levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Deep penetration of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP into multicellular tumor spheroids
ce:section.ce:para: Weigh 0.15 g agarose, dissolve in 10 mL PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4), prepare 1.5 % agarose solution, after autoclaving, draw 80 μL to 96-well plate while it is hot, let stand for 30 min, after the agarose is cooled and solidified, the cells can be inoculated. The logarithmic growth of MDA-MB-231 cells is inoculated in a 96-well plate containing agarose at a density of 1 × 104 / well, and allowed to stand in a cell culture tank, cultured for 48 h to form MDA-MB-231 cell tumor balls. After that, the culture medium is aspirated and discarded, and the complete incubation medium of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP under the conditions of pH 6.5 and pH 7.4 is added. After 24 h, the tumor balls are carefully sucked, washed twice with a pre-cooled PBS microscope, fixed with 4 % polyformaldehyde for 10 min, and the tumor balls are observed with fluorescence confocal tumor drugs.

For our study involving animal models, we obtained healthy female BALB/c nude mice, aged 8 weeks, from the Department of Laboratory Animals at Central South University, located in Changsha, China. In our efforts to establish tumor models, we meticulously injected 2 × 106 MDA-MB-231 cells subcutaneously into the dorsal flank of each mouse.

For in vivo magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) assessments, tumor-bearing mice were administered intravenously with solutions of CDBi,Mn-FA or CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP at a concentration of 50 μg/mL. Subsequent to injection, MRI scans were acquired at various time intervals to capture cross-sectional and coronal images of the tumor regions, providing insights into the distribution and localization of the contrast agents within the tumors. Additionally, a range of CDBi,Mn-FA solutions with varying manganese concentrations (0 to 100 μg/mL), each volume being 1.0 mL, were imaged under a 3 T MRI scanner.

CDBi,Mn-FA or CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP were administered intravenously to tumor-bearing mice, followed by euthanization at predetermined intervals to facilitate the extraction and subsequent analysis of tumor tissues. The harvested tissues underwent a comprehensive preparation process, including fixation to preserve structural integrity and permeabilization to enhance imaging agent penetration. Employing fluorescence microscopy, the prepared samples were imaged to exploit the unique fluorescence characteristics of the nanoparticles, enabling the monitoring of their distribution, localization, and relative concentration within the tumor matrix over time.

SD rats were adapted to the SPF environment for one week. CDBi,Mn-FA, CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP were injected into the tail vein. At 3 min, 5 min, 15 min, 30 min, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 10 h, and 24 h, 0.2 mL of blood was taken from the eyeball, and 0.4 mL of aqua regia was used for digestion for 4 h. ICP-OES was used to determine the content of bismuth in plasma.

BALB/c nude mice bearing tumors were randomly divided into six groups (n = 5 each): G1 = Control, G2 = RT (radiation therapy alone), G3 = CDBi-FA@CaP, G4 = RT + CDBi-FA@CaP, G5 = CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP, and G6 = RT + CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP NPs. The CDBi,Mn-FA and CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP were administered on the seventh day after subcutaneous tumor implantation, followed by radiotherapy 6 h post-administration. Tumor dimensions and body weights were meticulously recorded every two days. Tumor volume was determined using the formula: V = (width2 × length)/2. On day 16, comprehensive post-mortem analyses were conducted, including the collection of blood, tumor specimens, and major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) for subsequent histological and biochemical evaluations.

To construct survival curves for mice, with each group consisting of seven subjects (n = 7), survival time was meticulously tracked from the onset of treatment until the occurrence of predefined endpoint criteria, which included not only humane endpoints based on significant health deterioration or substantial loss of body weight but also reaching a tumor volume of 1500 cubic millimeters as a criterion for mortality. The Kaplan-Meier method was utilized to plot survival curves, depicting the percentage of surviving mice over time.

Upon completion of the therapeutic regimen, cardiac, hepatic, splenic, pulmonary, renal, and neoplastic tissues from each cohort of mice were meticulously harvested for histopathological evaluation. The collected specimens were first subjected to chemical fixation using formaldehyde to preserve morphological integrity. Subsequently, the tissues were embedded in paraffin wax and sectioned into thin slices. These sections then underwent a deparaffinization process and were rehydrated through a graded alcohol series. The slides were then stained with hematoxylin, a basic dye that binds to acidic substances such as nucleic acids, rendering the nuclei a distinct blue-purple hue. Following rinsing, the sections were counterstained with eosin, an acidic dye that preferentially stains basic components, such as cytoplasmic proteins, resulting in a pink to red appearance. The stained sections were finally dehydrated, cleared with xylene, and mounted with coverslips for microscopic examination.

For immunohistochemistry, tumor samples were fixed, embedded in paraffin, and sectioned. Sections were then processed for antigen retrieval, blocked, and incubated with primary antibodies against DNA damage marker γ-H2AX (Cat. ab11174) and cell proliferation marker Ki67 (Cat. ab15580). After primary antibody binding, appropriate secondary antibodies were applied for detection.

In parallel, immunofluorescence staining was performed to ascertain the levels of HIF1-α and ROS (Cat. D7008), along with the presence of apoptotic markers, TUNEL (Cat. G1504) and c-Caspase-3 (Cat. GB11532-50). Tissue sections were first deparaffinized and rehydrated, followed by antigen retrieval. Post-blocking, these sections were incubated with primary antibodies targeting HIF1-α and ROS for single-color fluorescence detection, and with antibodies against TUNEL and c-Caspase-3 for bicolor fluorescence analysis. Fluorescently labeled secondary antibodies were applied thereafter, and the sections were ultimately examined via fluorescence microscopy with Nikon Ni-E (Nikon, Minato, Japan) to determine the expression and localization of the targeted proteins.

Post-treatment serum samples from mice were analyzed for biochemical parameters, specifically assessing the levels of AST (aspartate aminotransferase), ALT (alanine aminotransferase), BUN (blood urea nitrogen), and Cre (creatinine). In parallel, complete blood counts were performed to measure red blood cell (RBC), white blood cell (WBC), and platelet (PLT) levels. The results were compared against the normal reference ranges for each parameter to evaluate the impact of the treatments on the mice's health.

SD rats were adapted to SPF environment for one week. CDBi,Mn-FA, CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP were injected into the tail vein. Urine and feces were collected at 0 h, 6 h, 12 h, 24 h, 36 h, 48 h, 60 h, and 72 h. 2 mL of urine was mixed with 4 mL of aqua regia for digestion for 12 h, and the content of bismuth in urine was determined by ICP-OES. The feces were added with an equal volume of water, stirred and mixed at 60 ℃, weighed 0.2 g, and mixed with 4 mL of aqua regia for digestion for 12 h. The content of bismuth in feces was determined by ICP-OES.

All the quantitative data were expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). Differences among groups were evaluated using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). Significance levels were set at *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, and ****p < 0.0001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and characterization of CDBi-FA, CDBi,Mn, and CDBi,Mn-FA
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Scheme 1, CDBi,Mn-FA was synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using glucose, manganese citrate, folic acid, and potassium bismuth citrate as raw materials. For control experiments, manganese citrate and folic acid were excluded, yielding bismuth-doped carbon dots (CDBi-FA) and bismuth-manganese co-doped non-targeted carbon dots (CDBi,Mn) through the same method. The surface morphology and chemical structure of the carbon dots were subsequently characterized.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) at various magnifications revealed that the three synthesized carbon dots exhibit a spherical morphology with a particle size of less than 5 nm and demonstrate good dispersion in solvents without significant aggregation (Fig. 1 A-C; Fig. S1). High-resolution TEM images indicated that CDBi,Mn-FA possesses high crystallinity. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the three types of carbon dots show a broad peak near 27° (Fig. 1D), indicating an amorphous carbon structure macroscopically.

The photophysical properties of the carbon dots were characterized using UV–visible spectroscopy and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The three carbon dots exhibit a dominant UV absorption peak at approximately 242 nm due to the π-π* transition of the C = C bond[37], while a broad peak near 342 nm may be attributed to the n-π* transition of the C = O bond or nitrogen-containing functional groups (Fig. 1E)[38]. Notably, the peak intensity around 342 nm is lower for CDBi,Mn due to the absence of nitrogen doping, compared to CDBi,Mn-FA and CDBi-FA. The PL spectrum of CDBi,Mn-FA shows a red shift in the maximum emission wavelength of the carbon dots as the excitation wavelength increases from 350 nm to 380 nm (Fig. 1F). At an excitation wavelength of 366 nm, all three carbon dots exhibit strong fluorescence emission at 450–460 nm (Fig. S2).

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were conducted to determine the chemical composition and surface functional groups of the three carbon dots. The FT-IR spectrum of CDBi,Mn exhibits characteristic absorption peaks corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O-H (3443 cm−1), C-H (2912 cm−1), CO (1629 cm−1), CC (1550 cm−1), –CH3 (1384 cm−1), and C-O (1079 cm−1) (Fig. 1G)[39–41]. Following nitrogen introduction via folic acid, the absorption bands for CDBi,Mn-FA and CDBi-FA at approximately 3443 cm−1, 2928 cm−1, 1702 cm−1, 1635 cm−1, 1384 cm−1, 1173 cm−1, and 1092 cm−1 correspond to O-H/N-H, C-H, O-CO, CC, –CH3, C-N, and C-O, respectively (Fig. 1H, I)[42]. The FT-IR spectra indicate successful nitrogen doping in the carbon dots.

XPS analysis was utilized to investigate the valence electron structure of elements in the carbon dots.[43] The XPS spectra show that CDBi,Mn-FA exhibits characteristic peaks for Mn and N elements, in contrast to CDBi-FA and CDBi,Mn (Fig. 1J). High-resolution XPS spectral fitting of relevant elements further detailed the functional groups of the carbon dots. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of CDBi-FA features four main peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.6 eV, 286.6 eV, and 288.7 eV, corresponding to C-C/CC, C-O, CO, and COO functional groups, respectively (Fig. S3A)[40,41,44,45]. The high-resolution C 1 s spectra of CDBi,Mn and CDBi,Mn-FA are similar (Fig. S4A and S5A). For the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S3B), three peaks at 531.2, 532.3, and 533.5 eV are attributed to CO, O-H, and C-O, respectively[46,47]. Notably, the O 1 s spectra of Mn-doped carbon dots exhibit additional peaks at 530.2 eV, attributed to the Mn-O structure [48] (Fig. S4B and S5B). The N 1 s spectra of CDBi-FA and CDBi,Mn-FA show peaks at 399, 401, and 401.3 eV, corresponding to pyridine N, pyrrole N, and graphite N, respectively[46] (Fig. S3C, S5C). High-resolution Bi 4f spectra at 159.6 and 164.8 eV correspond to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 (Fig. S3D, S4C, S5D)[49]. Mn 2p peaks, as seen in Figs. S4D and S5E, can be deconvoluted into Mn II (641.1 eV and 653 eV) and Mn III (642.4 eV and 653.8 eV) and their respective satellite peaks[48,50].

The structural stability of metal-doped carbon dots was examined by measuring the release of metal elements from CDBi,Mn-FA under different pH buffering conditions. The Bi element release was below the detection limit of ICP-OES (Fig. S6), indicating stable metal doping in the carbon dot structure and thereby endowing the carbon dots with multifunctional properties. Mn doping aims to enable the carbon dots to generate oxygen autonomously. To confirm this property, CDBi,Mn-FA was incubated with H2O2, resulting in substantial oxygen production within one minute (Fig. 1K). In contrast, CDBi-FA did not exhibit significant oxygen production, underscoring the advantage of Mn doping. CDBi,Mn-FA can achieve multiple cycles of oxygen production, exhibiting enzyme-like multiple turnover catalytic properties (Fig. 1L). Thus, CDBi,Mn-FA can catalyze O2 generation at high H2O2 levels within tumors through its catalase-like activity, alleviating hypoxia and enhancing radiation sensitization efficacy (Fig. 1L).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro tumor Targetability of CDBi,Mn-FA
ce:section.ce:para: Tumor cells, in contrast to normal cells, exhibit a high density of folate receptors on their surfaces. To exploit this feature, folic acid was incorporated as a raw material during the synthesis of the carbon dots, thereby endowing them with tumor-targeting properties. To validate this characteristic, uptake studies were performed using MDA-MB-231 tumor cells, which have high folate receptor expression, and HUVEC normal cells, which exhibit low folate receptor expression. The CCK8 assay confirmed that CDBi,Mn-FA possesses good biocompatibility, maintaining a cell viability greater than 75 % within a Bi concentration range of 50 μg/mL, which is comparable to CD-FA and CDBi-FA (Fig. S7).

Using the intrinsic fluorescence of the carbon dots, their cellular uptake behavior was investigated (Fig. 2 A). In MDA-MB-231 cells, the fluorescence signal of CDBi,Mn-FA was significantly stronger compared to CDBi,Mn However, following the addition of free folate to block receptors, the fluorescence of CDBi,Mn-FA markedly decreased, confirming the role of folate-mediated targeted delivery in the cellular uptake of the carbon dots. In HUVEC cells, which have low folate receptor expression, both types of carbon dots exhibited relatively weak uptake. Flow cytometry analysis (Fig. 2B, D) and subsequent quantitative analysis (Fig. 2C, E) of the fluorescence signal intensity within cells corroborated the fluorescence microscopy observations, demonstrating that CDBi,Mn-FA has a distinct tumor cell targeting ability.

To further substantiate this targeting capability, MDA-MB-231 cells were co-cultured with HUVEC cells, with MDA-MB-231 cells specifically labeled using the cell membrane dye Dil. Fluorescence co-localization analysis showed that CDBi,Mn-FA was predominantly taken up by MDA-MB-231 cells (Fig. 2F). These findings confirm that CDBi,Mn-FA can specifically recognize and selectively enter tumor cells with high folate receptor expression, providing a robust foundation for tumor-targeted therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro radiotherapy sensitization effect of CDBi,Mn-FA
ce:section.ce:para: Following the confirmation of the targeting characteristics of the carbon dots, we examined the radiosensitizing activity of CDBi,Mn-FA at the cellular level. Under both normoxic and hypoxic conditions, MDA-MB-231 (Fig. 3 A, B)., HuH7 (Fig. S8A, S8B).and SW480 (Fig. S9A, S9B). cells were co-incubated with different concentrations of CDBi,Mn-FA for 24 h, and cell viability was assessed using the CCK-8 assay. CDBi,Mn-FA did not exhibit significant anti-tumor activity at any concentration in the absence of radiation therapy. However, post-radiation therapy, CDBi,Mn-FA demonstrated excellent radiosensitizing ability, with anti-tumor activity positively correlated with both radiation dose and CDBi,Mn-FA concentration. Notably, under hypoxic conditions, CDBi,Mn-FA maintained excellent radiosensitization, likely due to its catalytic oxygen production capability.

To verify this hypothesis, we compared the radiosensitizing ability of CDBi-FA and CDBi,Mn-FA under 4 Gy irradiation, using CDBi-FA (without Mn) as a control. Under normoxic conditions, both carbon dots exhibited similar radiosensitizer activity (Fig. 3C, S8C, S9C). However, under hypoxic conditions, CDBi,Mn-FA showed superior radiosensitizing ability, especially at higher concentrations (Fig. 3D, S8D, S9D), confirming the advantage of CDBi,Mn-FA’s self-generated oxygen in radiosensitizing hypoxic tumors. The human-derived breast cancer organoid model also supports this conclusion, demonstrating that at the same concentration, CDBi,Mn-FA exhibits superior radiosensitizing ability compared to CDBi-FA (Fig. 3E, 3F, and Fig. S10).

The radiotherapy sensitization effect was further evaluated through cell cloning assays. Without a radiation sensitizer, 4 Gy irradiation had no significant effect on cells, whereas carbon dots containing Bi enhanced radiation sensitization. Folate-modified carbon dots showed superior effects due to tumor-targeted delivery, with CDBi,Mn-FA exhibiting the strongest inhibition of tumor cell clone formation (Fig. S11A). Semi-quantitative analysis of clone formation indicated that under normoxic conditions, both CDBi-FA and CDBi,Mn-FA significantly enhanced the radiotherapy killing effect on tumor cells (Fig. S11B, S11C). Under hypoxic conditions, the sensitization ability of CDBi-FA was notably weakened, whereas the activity of CDBi,Mn-FA remained largely unaffected.

Radiotherapy generates free radicals by interacting with intracellular water molecules, which attack DNA and cause cell damage or directly induce DNA double-strand breaks. Capitalized on this fact, we validated the mechanism by which carbon dots enhance tumor cell killing through radiotherapy at the cellular level. Intracellular free radicals were stained with the DC-FHDA assay kit (green fluorescence) and nuclei with the Hoechst assay kit (blue fluorescence). Confocal microscopy was used to observe intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels (Fig. 3G, Fig. S12). Without radiation, CDBi-FA and CDBi,Mn-FA did not significantly promote ROS generation, while radiation alone caused minimal ROS production. In contrast, combined treatment with CDBi-FA or CDBi,Mn-FA and radiation therapy resulted in significant intracellular fluorescence signals, particularly in the CDBi,Mn-FA group. Flow cytometry was used to measure ROS levels under normoxic and hypoxic conditions (Fig. S13), and quantitative analysis was performed (Fig. 3H, I). Consistent with the confocal microscopy observations, intracellular ROS production in the CDBi,Mn-FA group was significantly higher than in the CDBi-FA group post-radiation sensitization under both normoxic and hypoxic conditions.

Western blot analysis was employed to detect the DNA damage marker γ-H2AX and investigate the carbon dot-enhanced radiation-induced DNA damage in tumor cells. Under both normoxic and hypoxic conditions, γ-H2AX and ROS levels were consistent across treatment groups, with the radiotherapy + CDBi,Mn-FA group showing the highest levels. This is attributed to the dual factors of tumor targeting by folate modification and oxygen self-generation by the carbon dots, which alleviates hypoxia. The regulation of tumor hypoxia by carbon dots was further investigated by detecting the hypoxia marker protein HIF-1α (Fig. 3J-O). Under hypoxic conditions, HIF-1α expression significantly increased, and was further upregulated post-radiation due to oxygen consumption by radiotherapy. However, CDBi,Mn-FA treatment significantly downregulated HIF-1α expression, indicating that oxygen self-generation by the carbon dots alleviates tumor hypoxia.

Then, CDBi,Mn-FA was enCaPsulated within calcium phosphate nanoparticles through biomineralization to construct a tumor microenvironment-responsive drug delivery system, CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP. As depicted in Scheme 1, CDBi,Mn-FA was dispersed in a CaCl2 solution, then added to complete DMEM cell culture medium (containing 10 % FBS) and thoroughly stirred in the dark for 40 min to obtain CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP. The resulting reaction solution exhibited a clear Tyndall effect, indicating the successful synthesis of nanoparticles (Fig. S14). The particle size of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP is influenced by calcium ion concentration, reaction time, and carbon dot input (Figs. S15-S17). Previous studies confirmed that nanoparticles with a particle size around 100 nm exhibit good EPR effects[51]. Through prescription optimization, the prepared CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP nanoparticles achieved a particle size of 105 nm (Fig. 4 A), facilitating tumor targeting and enrichment.

Due to the abundance of carboxyl functional groups, the zeta potential of carbon dots is –23 mV (Fig. 4B). After surface adsorption of Ca2+ ions, the negative charge of the carbon dots significantly decreased, resulting in a final formulation zeta potential of −9 mV for CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP nanoparticles. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed that CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP appeared spherical with good dispersibility (Fig. 4C). EDX elemental scanning analysis demonstrated uniform distribution of Ca, P, Bi, and Mn throughout the nanoparticles (Fig. 4D), confirming the enCaPsulation of carbon dots by mineralized nanoparticles. Further analysis of elemental mass proportions revealed that Bi accounted for 0.8 % and Mn for 0.48 % (Fig. 4E, F). Dynamic monitoring of particle size indicated good colloidal stability of the nanoparticles in different media (Fig. 4G), beneficial for biomedical applications.

Previous studies confirmed that calcium phosphate mineralized nanoparticles possess acid-sensitive properties,[52,53] enabling response to tumor microenvironmental degradation. To verify this characteristic, CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP was incubated under buffer conditions of pH 7.4 and pH 6.5, with calcium ion content in the supernatant dynamically monitored to reflect nanoparticle degradation. Under pH 7.4 conditions, CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP released less than 10 % of Ca2+ within 4 h (Fig. 4H), and the centrifugal sediment at the bottom of Eppendorf tubes was significantly reduced (Fig. S18). However, at pH 6.5, Ca2+ release reached 80 % within 1 h. The disintegration of nanoparticles was observed more intuitively through solution state and microstructure. After incubation at pH 7.4, nanoparticles retained colloidal solution properties, whereas at pH 6.5, the solution became clear (inset in Fig. 4H). TEM observations showed that after incubation at pH 7.4 for 30 min, CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP retained its complete nanoparticle structure (Fig. 4I). In contrast, after incubation at pH 6.5 for 5 min, nanoparticle morphology became blurred, with some nanoparticles fragmenting; extending the time to 30 min resulted in nearly complete disintegration, with released carbon dots observable in the field of view. In addition, we examined the fluorescence behavior of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP under different pH conditions. When incubated at pH 7.4, the fluorescence intensity was relatively low, likely due to the calcium phosphate shell partially shielding the carbon dots, preventing excitation light from effectively reaching the encapsulated carbon dots and thus reducing the fluorescence. However, at the same concentration, the fluorescence intensity increased at pH 6.5, indicating that the calcium phosphate shell largely degrades under acidic conditions, allowing the fluorescence to recover (Fig. S19).

The disintegration and carbon dot release from mineralized nanoparticles were further supported by measuring catalytic activity. Under pH 7.4 conditions, encapsulated carbon dots could not contact H2O2, losing their catalase-like catalytic activity (Fig. 4J). However, at pH 6.5, released carbon dots restored their catalytic activity almost entirely. These data indicate that CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP has a stable structure under physiological conditions, conducive to long circulation and tumor enrichment via the EPR effect. Upon entering tumor tissue, nanoparticles rapidly disintegrate and release carbon dots in response to the slightly acidic environment, facilitating deep tumor infiltration and enhancing hypoxia regulation and radiosensitization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo tumor targeting delivery and intratumoral deep penetration of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the in Vivo tumor targeting and intratumoral penetration of CDBi,MN-FA@CaP, a subcutaneous tumor transplantation model of MDA-MB-231 was constructed. CDBi,Mn-FA and CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP were injected into the tail vein, and the enrichment of nanoparticles in tumor tissue was observed through the intrinsic fluorescence of carbon dots. Both CDBi,Mn-FA and CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP exhibited the highest tumor enrichment 4 h post-administration, with CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP showing significantly stronger fluorescence signals compared to CDBi,Mn-FA (Fig. 5 A). Fluorescence quantification confirmed that the fluorescence intensity of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP was significantly higher than that of CDBi,Mn-FA at both 4 and 12-hour time points (Fig. 5B).

To verify the deep penetration of nanoparticles into tumors, a three-dimensional (3D) tumor spheroid model of MDA-MB-231 was constructed. Using the spontaneous fluorescence of carbon dots, the deep penetration of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP was observed under confocal microscopy. Under pH 7.4 conditions, the green fluorescence of carbon dots showed weak fluorescence intensity in the tumor spheroid (Fig. 5C). However, under pH 6.5 conditions, which simulate the tumor microenvironment, the fluorescence signal of the nanoparticles in the 3D tumor spheroid was strong and evenly distributed. Fluorescence quantification indicated that as the pH of the microenvironment decreased from 7.4 to 6.5, the permeability of nanoparticles inside the tumor increased threefold (Fig. 5D). Thus, the acid-sensitive disintegration of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP facilitates the exposure and deep penetration of CDBi,Mn-FA.

The manganese (Mn) element in carbon dots provides magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) capabilities. Therefore, the in vivo tumor penetration ability of carbon dots was evaluated using MRI T1 signals. In solution, as the concentration of CDBi,Mn-FA increased, the intensity of the T1 relaxation phase MRI signal also increased (Fig. S20), confirming the nuclear magnetic sensitization ability of the nanoparticles. After tail vein injection of the nanoparticles, T1 MRI imaging was performed at various time points. Results indicated that, compared to muscle tissue, the signal within the tumor gradually increased and was evenly distributed in the tumor tissue (Fig. 5E, F).

Furthermore, the tumor enrichment of CDBi,Mn-FA and CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP was compared using MRI T1 imaging. The findings revealed that in tumor-bearing mouse models, CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP achieved higher tumor enrichment than CDBi,Mn-FA when injected intravenously at the same concentration (Fig. 5G, 5H). These results confirm that CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP possesses superior tumor enrichment capability compared to CDBi,Mn-FA, and its degradation under slightly acidic tumor conditions further promotes the exposure and deep penetration of carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo Anti-Tumor efficacy evaluation of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the in Vivo anti-tumor efficacy of the nanoparticles, tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into six groups. When the tumor volume reached approximately 100 mm3, the following treatments were administered: G1 = Control, G2 = RT (radiotherapy), G3 = CDBi-FA@CaP, G4 = CDBi-FA@CaP + RT, G5 = CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP, and G6 = RT + CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP. During the treatment period, tumor size was dynamically monitored (Fig. 6 A). Post-treatment, tumor tissues were excised and weighed (Fig. 6B, Fig. S21) to evaluate the anti-tumor efficacy of each treatment group. The results showed no significant therapeutic effects in both the radiotherapy alone and untreated nanoparticle groups. However, the combination of nanoparticles and radiotherapy exhibited significant tumor suppression effects, demonstrating the radiosensitizer activity of the nanoparticles. Among the groups, the RT + CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP group had the best therapeutic effect, with complete tumor elimination observed in 2 out of 5 mice (a cure rate of 40 %), and significant tumor size reduction in the remaining 3 mice. This enhanced effect is attributed to the tumor-targeted enrichment, deep penetration, and self-oxygen production capabilities of the nanoparticles.

Survival status of each group of mice post-treatment was observed to evaluate long-term efficacy (Fig. 6C). The survival rate of the RT + CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP group reached 100 % within 45 days, while all other treatment groups had succumbed by the end of the observation period. To further elucidate the therapeutic effect, pathological examination of the tumor tissues post-treatment was performed (Fig. 6D-F). c-Caspase-3 (red) and TUNEL (green) staining were used to evaluate cell apoptosis, revealing weak fluorescence signals in tumor tissues following radiotherapy alone or nanotherapies. However, fluorescence signals were significantly enhanced after combined radiotherapy and nanoparticle treatments, with the RT + CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP group showing the strongest apoptotic signal. H&E staining results corroborated these findings, further confirming the excellent anti-tumor effect of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP combined with radiotherapy. Ki67 is a nuclear protein that indicates cell proliferation and is an important marker for measuring tumor growth rate. Immunohistochemical results showed that the expression level of Ki67 in tumor tissues was lowest following RT + CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP treatment, indicating the most significant inhibition of tumor cell proliferation (Fig. 6G, H).

Further exploration of the radiosensitization mechanism of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP was conducted (Fig. 6G-I, Figs. S22, S23). ROS levels in tumor tissues were measured using fluorescence labeling. Under radiotherapy conditions, significant fluorescence enhancement was observed in tumor tissues. After sensitization with combined nanoparticle radiotherapy, fluorescence intensity further increased, with the RT + CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP group showing the most significant effect (Fig. 6G, Fig. S22). Immunohistochemical analysis showed that γ-H2AX expression was significantly upregulated in tumor tissues post-radiotherapy, with the RT + CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP group showing the most pronounced effect, indicating severe nuclear damage (Fig. 6G, I). These results confirm that radiotherapy exerts anti-tumor effects by generating free radicals and inducing DNA damage.

To elucidate the in Vivo self-oxygen generation activity of the nanoparticles, HIF-1α expression levels in tumor tissues were measured using immunofluorescence staining (Fig. 6G, S23). Tumor tissues in the control group showed certain immunofluorescence, confirming the hypoxic characteristics of the tumor microenvironment. Following radiotherapy, the fluorescence signal further increased due to oxygen consumption by radiotherapy. Notably, the RT + CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP treatment significantly reduced HIF-1α levels, attributed to the manganese-doped carbon dots decomposing H2O2 in the tumor microenvironment and releasing oxygen, thereby alleviating hypoxia in the central region of the tumor and enhancing radiotherapy efficacy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Safety assessment and in vivo excretion of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP
ce:section.ce:para: In parallel with confirming the efficacy of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP, we conducted a comprehensive study of its in Vivo biosafety to evaluate its potential for clinical translation. Compared to the PBS control group, no abnormal changes in body weight were observed in any treatment group of mice (Fig. 7 A). Liver and kidney function indicators remained within the normal range (Fig. 7B-7E). Complete blood cell counts from mice showed no abnormal changes in red blood cells, white blood cells, or platelets (Fig. 7F-7H). Post-treatment, major organs from the mice were collected and subjected to H&E staining, revealing no abnormalities in any organs (Fig. 7I). These results collectively confirm the high biocompatibility and safety of the nanoparticles.

To further evaluate the in Vivo metabolic behavior of the nanoparticles, we injected equal doses of CDBi,Mn-FA or CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP into rats via the tail vein and collected plasma at various time points to measure Bi content via ICP-OES, reflecting the blood concentration of CDBi,Mn-FA (Fig. 7J). CDBi,Mn-FA was rapidly eliminated from the bloodstream, likely due to its small particle size, facilitating filtration by the glomeruli. In contrast, the half-life of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP was significantly extended, demonstrating that mineralized nanoparticle enCaPsulation allows for prolonged circulation of carbon dots. Urine and fecal samples collected from rats at different time points were analyzed to determine drug excretion (Fig. 7K, 7L). Fecal excretion rates of both CDBi,Mn-FA and CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP were similar, with a cumulative excretion rate of about 20 % within 72 h. However, renal excretion rates differed significantly; CDBi,Mn-FA exhibited a much higher renal excretion rate than CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP. This is attributed to the smaller particle size of CDBi,Mn-FA, enabling filtration through renal tubules and direct excretion in urine. Three days post-injection, about 60 % of CDBi,Mn-FA was metabolized out of the body, whereas only 40 % of CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP was excreted, indicating that while small particle size CDBi,Mn-FA is more readily metabolized, CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP nanoparticles are gradually excreted over time (Fig. 7M). Additionally, quantitative analysis of carbon dot concentration in major organs three days post-injection (Fig. 7N) showed only slight accumulation in liver and kidney tissues, further supporting the safety of the nanoparticles for in Vivo applications.

This study successfully developed CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP, a dynamic conformational nanoparticle designed to address the limitations of conventional radiotherapy through tumor microenvironment-responsive characteristics. CDBi,Mn-FA was synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method, with comprehensive characterization of its structure, morphology, and optical properties. The carbon dots core exhibits robust radiosensitization and oxygen generation capabilities, augmented by folic acid for targeted tumor cell delivery. CDBi,Mn-FA was mineralized into CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP to enhance in Vivo circulation and tumor accumulation, with subsequent degradation in acidic tumor environments releasing smaller particles for deep tissue penetration. Validation through immunofluorescence staining, magnetic resonance imaging, and in vitro 3D tumor models confirmed CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP's efficacy in tumor targeted enrichment and deep penetration. Significant tumor suppression and improved survival rates were observed in tumor-bearing mice treated with CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP. The nanoparticle demonstrated high biocompatibility and efficient excretion, supporting its potential for clinical translation. Overall, CDBi,Mn-FA@CaP represents a promising advancement in multifunctional nanoparticle radiosensitizers, offering a strategic approach to overcoming radiotherapy resistance and enhancing treatment outcomes for patients with malignant tumors. Moreover, this study provides an innovative strategy for targeted tumor delivery.
All animal experiments in this study were conducted in accordance with the ARRIVE guidelines and were approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of Central South University.
